explanation: "Final State shall be reached by prop_0 to prop_1" means that at some point in the future, prop_0 holds, and then eventually, afterwards, prop_1 holds. Thus, it can be expressed as: eventually, prop_0 occurs and then eventually after that, prop_1 occurs.

In natural language, this implies that whenever prop_1 occurs, it must be preceded (possibly immediately or at some point in the past) by prop_0. In LTL, using 'by' typically encodes a response relationship: for every occurrence of prop_1, prop_0 must have happened before or at the same time. In LTL, this is expressed as globally: if prop_1 holds, then previously or currently prop_0 has held, which in standard future LTL is written as globally: if prop_1, then in the past or present, prop_0 held, but standard LTL does not have past operators. To encode this in standard future LTL, it's common to use: G(prop_1 -> F prop_0), but this is future-looking; for the strict 'by' sense, with only future LTL, the best translation is that prop_1 cannot occur until prop_0 has occurred, i.e., !prop_1 U prop_0, and then prop_1 can occur any time afterward. But under usual interpretations, response is: G(prop_1 -> F prop_0), but this translates back to always: if prop_1, then prop_0 will eventually happen. But with only future LTL, a common pattern is G(prop_1 -> F prop_0).

The phrase 'prop_0 by prop_1' usually means that 'prop_0' must become true at or before 'prop_1' becomes true. In LTL, this can be captured as: either 'prop_1' never occurs (in which case there is no constraint), or if 'prop_1' is ever true, then 'prop_0' must have been true at the same time or earlier. This is expressed as 'not prop_1 until prop_0' or ('prop_1' -> F[0:1] prop_0), but using only allowed LTL operators, we use: G (prop_1 -> F[0:1] prop_0).
The statement means that 'Final State' is a state where prop_1 and prop_2 hold, and the system should eventually reach that condition. In LTL, this is captured by 'F (prop_1 & prop_2)'.

The statement means that 'prop_0' must happen before 'prop_1'. In LTL, this is expressed by saying it is never the case that 'prop_1' occurs before 'prop_0' has occurred. A standard encoding is to say that until 'prop_1' becomes true, it is always the case that 'prop_0' has not happened yet. But since we want 'prop_0' to occur before 'prop_1', it is succinct to say: if 'prop_1' ever occurs, 'prop_0' must have held at some point before. That is, globally, if 'prop_1' holds, then previously (historically) 'prop_0' held sometime earlier. In standard future LTL, this is most accurately captured (for safety properties) as: 'not prop_1 until prop_0'.

The phrase 'prop_0 by prop_1 to prop_2' can be interpreted as 'once prop_1 happens, prop_0 should hold continuously until prop_2 holds.' This matches the LTL 'release' pattern, but more commonly the 'until' operator is used for such constraints. Thus, from whenever prop_1 becomes true, prop_0 should hold at least until (and including) the point where prop_2 becomes true.

The requirement 'prop_2 by prop_0 to prop_1' usually encodes that if prop_0 holds, prop_2 must occur before or when prop_1 happens. That is, starting from when prop_0 holds, prop_2 must hold at least once until prop_1 occurs (or at the time prop_1 occurs). If prop_1 never happens, prop_2 must eventually happen after prop_0. This is expressed as: 'Whenever prop_0 holds, prop_2 must hold at least once before or when prop_1 holds.'
The specification requires that the state in which prop_0 holds is eventually reached. This can be captured in LTL as 'eventually prop_0', which is 'F prop_0'.

The requirement states that 'prop_0' must be reached (i.e., eventually true) during the course of the system, and this must happen no later than when 'prop_1' first becomes true. In temporal logic, this is captured by saying that either 'prop_0' is reached before 'prop_1' ever occurs, or, if 'prop_1' occurs, then 'prop_0' must have already occurred or occur at the same time. This is succinctly captured by the 'until' operator: '¬prop_1 U prop_0', which states that 'prop_1' cannot occur until 'prop_0' has occurred (if ever). Alternatively, once 'prop_1' becomes true, 'prop_0' must already be true or must have become true before. This is usually expressed as 'F prop_0' and '¬prop_1 U prop_0', but the most precise translation for 'be reached by' is: 'prop_1 -> F[0:1] prop_0', meaning if 'prop_1' is ever true, 'prop_0' is true at the latest at the same time. But the accepted temporal encoding is: 'prop_1 -> (prop_0 | X prop_0)', i.e., when 'prop_1' holds, 'prop_0' must hold now or in the next state.

The formula requires that 'prop_1' must eventually become true at some point, meaning the final state should be reached by the condition expressed by 'prop_1'. In LTL, this is expressed as 'eventually prop_1'.
